tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: The legal existence of the video rental industry (e.g., Blockbuster, Netflix) is historically rooted in the 'first sale' doctrine upheld in the 1984 'Betamax' case. Identify the landmark 2013 Supreme Court case that expanded this doctrine to imported goods, involving a Thai math student who resold textbooks. Name the student and the publisher that sued him. Furthermore, summarize the conflicting industry reactions to this 2013 ruling: specifically, what advantage for American consumers was cited by Gary Shapiro of the Consumer Electronics Association, and what negative economic consequence regarding foreign markets was predicted by the Business Software Alliance?

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: The legal existence of the video rental industry (e.g., Blockbuster, Netflix) is historically rooted in the 'first sale' doctrine upheld in the 1984 'Betamax' case. Identify the landmark 2013 Supreme Court case that expanded this doctrine to imported goods, involving a Thai math student who resold textbooks. Name the student and the publisher that sued him. Furthermore, summarize the conflicting industry reactions to this 2013 ruling: specifically, what advantage for American consumers was cited by Gary Shapiro of the Consumer Electronics Association, and what negative economic consequence regarding foreign markets was predicted by the Business Software Alliance?
checklist_width: CEA/Shapiro Reaction: Americans can shop worldwide for copyrighted content / won't be 'chumps' paying highest prices / access to lower overseas prices
BSA Reaction: Authors/Companies will have little incentive to price programs for foreign markets / Domestic prices will be undercut by re-imports / Foreign consumers might be deprived of products
checklist_depth: Target Case: Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons
Logic Proof: Connects the 2013 textbook resale case to the 1984 Betamax/First Sale doctrine that legalizes video rentals.
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by anchoring the query in the history of the 'Video rental shop' domain (Betamax case) to identify a legally analogous modern case (Kirtsaeng). It uses Wide Logic by requiring the retrieval of specific names (litigants) and the aggregation of contrasting arguments from two distinct industry organizations (CEA and BSA) found within the details of the event.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times

rounds.answer_a:
<minimax:tool_call>
<invoke="search">
["2013 Supreme Court first sale doctrine imported goods Thai math student textbooks"]
rounds.answer_b:
The landmark 2013 Supreme Court case is **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.**, which expanded the **first sale doctrine** to imported goods, ruling 6-3 that the doctrine applies to copyrighted materials lawfully made abroad and resold in the U.S. without publisher permission[3]. The Thai math student was **Supap Kirtsaeng**, who bought textbooks in Thailand at lower prices and resold them on eBay in the U.S. for profit; the publisher that sued him was **John Wiley & Sons, Inc.**[3].

Industry reactions to the ruling were sharply divided. **Gary Shapiro**, then-CEO of the **Consumer Electronics Association** (CEA), hailed it as a victory for consumers, citing the advantage of **lower prices and greater access to goods** like electronics and media, enabling Americans to resell imported devices without copyright restrictions—echoing the Betamax case's protection of time-shifting via VCRs[1][2][3]. Conversely, the **Business Software Alliance** (BSA) warned of severe negative economic consequences, predicting that the decision would **shrink U.S. companies' foreign markets** by allowing unchecked gray-market imports of software and other products, undermining pricing strategies and export revenues abroad[3].

This built on the 1984 **Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios** (Betamax) decision, which upheld home video recording under fair use, fostering the rental industry like Blockbuster and early Netflix[1][2]. Kirtsaeng's expansion prevented publishers from using copyright to block resales, promoting free markets but alarming content industries[3].

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] USPTO, Comments of the Consumer Electronics Association.  
[2] NTIA, Comments of the Consumer Electronics Association.  
[3] SCOTUSblog, Impression Products v. Lexmark Amicus Brief (citing Kirtsaeng).  
[4] EFF, CES 2007: What Gary Said.

John Wiley & Sons), the student (Supap Kirtsaeng), and the publisher. It also accurately summarized the specific arguments from Gary Shapiro (consumer access/lower prices) and the BSA (harm to foreign market pricing strategies) while adhering to the word count constraint.
